name: Actor Attribution And Avatar Memory
summary: Track who created, consumed, transformed, and still remembers information across Calder, agents, avatars, and external creators.

steps.label: Imported media enters as content items with source metadata.
explanation: YouTube, reading, listening, Prime, Spotify, and YouTube Music records keep their source-system identity.
The second brain does not treat watched content as authored by Calder; it treats it as consumed by Calder and created by the external creator when metadata is available.

steps.label: Humans, agents, avatars, creators, authors, and systems become actors.
explanation: The actor registry is the identity layer for attribution.
Calder/CJ, Calder.agents(), Prototype-Calder-Avatar, channels, authors, and future avatar instances are represented by the same actor shape.

steps.label: Hapa artifacts are marked as Calder plus agent-created outputs.
explanation: Wiki pages, cards, source artifacts, and Hapa songs are treated as Hapa-authored or curated outputs.
The ledger preserves the co-creative relationship between Calder and the AI agents that helped produce the artifacts.

steps.label: Agents register consumed context and active memory state.
explanation: When an agent queries the second brain, returned items, chunks, and articles become encountered information for that agent.
The memory record starts as session-active until a later process confirms durable avatar memory or marks it evicted.

steps.label: Summaries, plans, products, and apps are registered as created derivations.
explanation: A product plan or built application should point back to the source items and agents that shaped it.
This makes the Prototype-Calder-Avatar capable of explaining not just what it knows, but how knowledge became applied work.

card.description: Separates creator, consumer, curator, agent, and avatar memory roles across the second brain.
card.effect: Every retrieved or created object can answer who created it, who consumed it, who transformed it, and which avatar or agent can still use it.
card.risk: Thin source metadata can overstate authorship; confidence and source-system fields must remain visible.
